coredata.dc:title: Facile off-on fluorescence biosensing of human papillomavirus using DNA probe coupled with sunflower seed shells carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Xinxin
Liu, Xinjian
Wu, Meng
Ma, Yanmei
Xu, Xiaoyu
Chen, Ligang
Niu, Na
coredata.dc:description: We report a fluorescent “off-on” platform based on carbon dots (CDs) and DNA probe to achieve trace detection of human papillomavirus 16 (HPV 16). Initially, the CDs was synthesized by simple two-step method, using biomass sunflower seed shells as precursor, doped with L-arginine and passivated by polyethyleneimine (PEI) to obtain positively charged biomass CDs that had the merits of high fluorescence intensity, green synthesis and good stability. Then, the DNA probe was prepared by modifying single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) with 4-(4-dimethylaminobenzazo) benzoyl (Dabcyl) fluorescence quenching agent. Due to electrostatic effect and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism, DNA probe can cause fluorescence quenching of the prepared CDs. Since HPV 16 bound to the DNA probe followed a strict base complementation pairing rule, it turned on the fluorescence of CDs by separating the DNA probe from the CDs. The limit of detection (LOD = 0.47 nM) and the linear range (0.5–150 nM) of the probe for HPV 16 were favorable. The recovery of HPV 16 in urine and cervical swabs were 96.6%-113.3% and 95.7%-103.9%. This method can achieve highly specific detection of the DNA sequences of target virus without complicated operation and expensive equipment, which is of great significance for biochemical analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: HPV 16
Sunflower seed shells
Carbon dots
DNA probe
Urine and cervical swabs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental procedure for the detection of HPV 16
Analysis of HPV 16 in urine and cervical swabs
Optimization of synthesis conditions of SCDs
Selectivity analysis for detection of HPV 16 assays
Analysis of HPV16 in actual urine and cervical swab samples
Fluorescence quenching and recovery mechanism of SCDs

Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the strategy for HPV 16 analysis.

(a) TEM images (inset: HRTEM image) of SCDs, (b) XRD pattern, (c) FTIR spectrum of SCDs, (d) Ultraviolet absorption spectrum (curve 1), fluorescence excitation (curve 2) and emission spectrum (curve 3) of SCDs (insert: optical images of SCDs under daylight and UV light), (e) The 3D fluorescence contour map of SCDs, (f) The fluorescence spectra of the SCDs under different excitation wavelengths.
(a) XPS of the SCDs, (b) The C1 s analysis, (c) O1 s analysis, (d) N1 s analysis of the SCDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of SCDs interacting with different substances, (b) The fluorescence spectra of SCDs (Ex = 350 nm, Em = 467 nm) after reaction with various concentrations of D-DNA solution, (c) The linear relationship for SCDs and D-DNA, where [D-DNA] = 10, 25, 40, 60, 80, 100, 140, 175, 225, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450 nmoL·L−1, (d) The fluorescence spectra of SCDs (Ex = 350 nm, Em = 467 nm) after reaction with various concentrations of HPV 16 (inset: fluorescence recovery of SCDs under UV light), (e) The linear relationship for SCDs and HPV 16, where [HPV 16] = 0.5, 10, 25, 50, 70, 90, 110, 125, 140, 150, 160, 175, 200 nmoL·L−1, (f) Fluorescence response of SCDs-D-DNA probe to varieties of substances. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of SCDs-D-DNA in the absence and presence of HPV16 or other substances. Other substances include 10 μM different number of base mismatched of ssDNA, anions, amino acids and biomolecules.
(a) The UV–Vis spectra of SCDs and SCDs-D-DNA solution, (b) Zeta potential changes of CDs, SCDs, D-DNA and SCDs-D-DNA, (c) Fluorescence lifetime spectra of SCDs and (d) SCDs-D-DNA.
Comparison of distinct assay methods for HPV (n = 3).

row.entry: Zn-doped MoS2 QDs/ Reductive Cu(I) particles
Multiplex paper/ Pyrrolidinyl peptide nucleic acid/ AgNPs aggregation

head.ce:title: Facile off-on fluorescence biosensing of human papillomavirus using DNA probe coupled with sunflower seed shells carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization
Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a fluorescent “off-on” platform based on carbon dots (CDs) and DNA probe to achieve trace detection of human papillomavirus 16 (HPV 16). Initially, the CDs was synthesized by simple two-step method, using biomass sunflower seed shells as precursor, doped with L-arginine and passivated by polyethyleneimine (PEI) to obtain positively charged biomass CDs that had the merits of high fluorescence intensity, green synthesis and good stability. Then, the DNA probe was prepared by modifying single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) with 4-(4-dimethylaminobenzazo) benzoyl (Dabcyl) fluorescence quenching agent. Due to electrostatic effect and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism, DNA probe can cause fluorescence quenching of the prepared CDs. Since HPV 16 bound to the DNA probe followed a strict base complementation pairing rule, it turned on the fluorescence of CDs by separating the DNA probe from the CDs. The limit of detection (LOD = 0.47 nM) and the linear range (0.5–150 nM) of the probe for HPV 16 were favorable. The recovery of HPV 16 in urine and cervical swabs were 96.6%-113.3% and 95.7%-103.9%. This method can achieve highly specific detection of the DNA sequences of target virus without complicated operation and expensive equipment, which is of great significance for biochemical analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Human papillomavirus (HPV) is a DNA virus that can cause the proliferation of squamous epithelium in the human skin mucosa [1]. More than 130 types of HPV have been isolated, and different types of HPV have different clinical manifestations. Persistent infection with high-risk HPV 16 and HPV 18 are the leading causes of cervical, rectal, oral, tonsil and other cancers. The incidence of cervical cancer is particularly high among women. In recent years, these diseases have tended to strike young people. Cervical cancer affects approximately half a million people worldwide annual year, and it has a very high mortality rate [2]. However, if HPV levels in the body can be detected early, cancers can be determined and prevented in time, thus testing for HPV is of great importance in cancer prevention and clinical diagnosis.

Various methods have been used for HPV detection at present, including traditional diagnostic techniques based on cytology (Pap smear or thin-layer pretreatment) [3], nested polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [4], real-time PCR [5], electrochemiluminescence immunoassay [6] and microfluidic chip technology [7], etc. However, among these methods, the Pap smear technique is limited by the high requirements for accurate determination and technique. PCR often produces false positive results during detection. Electrochemiluminescence immunoassay is usually characterized by short luminescence process, high background and instrument failure rate. The cost of microfluidic chip technology and technical requirements for operators are high. Therefore, with the increasing demands for nucleic acid diagnosis and development, there is an urgent need to develop low-cost, easy to operate and rapid detection methods for HPV.

The simple and rapid fluorescence method is gradually attracting interest because it can avoid many disadvantages of the above methods. However, in terms of fluorescent material selection, traditional ones such as organic fluorescent molecular materials [8], rare earth-doped inorganic fluorescent materials [9], semiconductor quantum dots [10] and metal nanoclusters [11] are often limited by complex reaction steps, high reagent costs and high toxicity when used for DNA sensing. Indeed, carbon dots (CDs), as novel fluorescent nanomaterials, have multiple advantages in the biomedical field with their excellent optical properties, biocompatibility, stability and water dispersibility. CDs have unique tunable photoluminescence (PL) properties, and their PL stability and chemical inertness can fully meet the requirements of nanoprobes in biosensing [12,13]. Furthermore, CDs can be used as a molecular imaging probe in fluorescence sensing, biological imaging and cancer diagnosis [14,15]. For example, CDs has been successfully applied to fluorescence-based discrimination of broad-spectrum cancer cells from high-contrast normal cells. [16]. In recent years, the synthesis of biomass CDs from renewable, low-cost, environmentally friendly, simple and inexpensive synthetic conditions using biomass waste as precursors has become increasingly prevalent [17,18]. Owing to the mounts of functional groups on their surface, they provide abundant modification sites for the construction of effective nanoprobes for biosensing. To achieve precise determination of DNA for specific target sequences, enzymatic reactions and antigen–antibody specific recognition often receive limitation, while DNA base-pairing hybridization technique is the excellent option [19,20]. Consequently, the construction of SCDs-DNA probe biosensor has a broad application prospect. In addition, the biosensor has the advantages of strong specificity, rapid detection ability and easy miniaturization.

Motivated by the above study, herein, we constructed a DNA sensing strategy for specific and reliable fluorescence detection of HPV 16 DNA based on sunflower seed shells CDs (SCDs). The biomass carbon dots used in this experiment was synthesized by simple hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 ). Specifically, waste sunflower seed shells and L-arginine were used as carbon and nitrogen sources, while polyethyleneimine (PEI) was used as passivating agent. Sunflower seed shells, whose main components are lignin and cellulose, are ideal precursors for the synthesis of high additional value SCDs. Meanwhile, the DNA probe modified by Dabcyl dark fluorescent quenching agent at the 3′ end (D-DNA) was constructed. The negatively charged D-DNA was attracted to the prepared SCDs by electrostatic interaction, and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) led to turn off the fluorescence of SCDs. In contrast, the introduction of HPV 16 weakened this effect and turned on the fluorescence of SCDs owing to specific base pairing of DNA. A strategy for the detection of specific DNA sequences was developed on this basis, which can be applied to the analysis of actual samples of urine and cervical swabs.
All reagents and instrumentation for this experiment are available in Electronic Support Materials (ESM).

Waste sunflower seed shells were used as a carbon source and L-arginine as a nitrogen source to prepare nitrogen-doped SCDs. Sunflower seed shells were dried, pounded and mechanically polished to make powder. The above powder (1.0 g) was weighed in a beaker. L-arginine (0.2 g) and distilled water (20 mL) were added and mixed well and transferred to PTFE liner. The PTFE liner was incorporated into the reactor and tightened. The reaction kettle was placed in a blast drying oven and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the supernatant was centrifuged and filtered using a microporous membrane of 0.22 μM diameter. Then, the obtained light yellow filtrate was dialyzed through a 3500 MWCO dialysis bag for three days and SCDs solid powder was obtained by rotary evaporation and freeze-drying, weighed and stored in the dark outside at 4 °C.

Briefly, the prepared SCDs powder (0.01 g) was placed in a round bottom flask and 10 μL of polyethyleneimine (PEI) was added in the water bath at 60 °C and stirred for 2 h to obtain yellow caramelized SCDs for later use.

The fluorescence quantum yields of SCDs at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm were calculated according to following equation [21].
Q
CD
=
Q R
∙
I
CD
I R
∙
A R
A
CD
∙
η
CD
2
η
R
2

Q in the formula represents the quantum yield, I is the fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript R denotes the reference in this formula. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 54%) solution was chosen as the reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental procedure for the detection of HPV 16
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the quenching process of fluorescence induction of SCDs by DNA modified by Dabcyl (D-DNA). First, the prepared SCDs solution (50 μL) was added in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS Buffer, pH = 7.4, 20 mM), and different concentrations of D-DNA (200 μL) solution were added at 37 °C. The system solution was diluted to 1 mL with PBS buffer and then shaken and incubated for 35 min at 350 nm for fluorescence detection.

Next, HPV 16 was detected under optimal fluorescence off conditions by adding 50 μL SCDs solution, 200 μL D-DNA (1.5 μM) and various concentrations of HPV 16 (200 μL) solution in a 2 mL centrifuge tube and diluting to 1 mL with PBS buffer. The solution was incubated for 40 min and then subjected to fluorescence detection. The experiment was repeated three times for each group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of HPV 16 in urine and cervical swabs
ce:section.ce:para: The actual samples tested in this study were HPV 16-positive urine and cervical swabs obtained from Qinggang Chinese Medicine Hospital. Urine sample was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min and the supernatant was removed [22]. The remaining sample suspension containing cell debris was washed and stored at 4 °C. HPV DNA was extracted from urine and cervical swabs using Ezup Column Virus DNA Purification Kit. The extracted DNA solution was diluted 10 times with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 20 mM) and stored at 95 °C for 5 min and cooled at 0 °C for 5 min. The procedure for detecting urine and cervical swabs samples was the same as the above experiments. Fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was measured at 350 nm excitation wavelength. Similarly, the HPV 16 assay described above was used for the detection of spiked samples. The experiment was repeated three times for each group.

The morphology and size of SCDs can be seen from transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In Fig. 2 a, most SCDs dispersed well and the size distribution of SCDs ranges from 1.3 nm to 4.7 nm, with an average diameter of 3.3 nm (Fig. S1). The illustration shows high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) graphic of SCDs with a lattice spacing value of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with graphene's in-plane lattice spacing (100 faces) [26].

The XRD diagram of SCDs shows the crystal morphology and internal structure (Fig. 2b), the wide diffraction peak of SCDs is located at 2θ = 22.1°, which points to plane C(002) [27]. FTIR analysis confirmed that there were nitrogen functionalization on the outer surface of SCDs and a variety of functional groups of sunflower seed shell, with a small amount of amorphous properties [28].

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of SCDs (Fig. 2c) shows the functional groups on the surface, where the peaks at about 3398 cm−1, 2790 cm−1 belong to O–H, C–H stretching vibrations, respectively, and the peak at 1590 cm−1 belongs to C = O group, indicating that the surface of SCDs is rich in hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, and the peak at 1440 cm−1 is N–H bending vibration indicating the increase of N element content in SCDs, the peak at 1360 cm−1 is C-O-C symmetric stretching vibration, and the peak at 1070 cm−1 is C-O/C-N stretching vibration [29].

UV–visible spectra, fluorescence excitation spectra, emission spectra and three-dimensional fluorescence spectra were measured to study the optical properties of SCDs. As shown in Fig. 2d (Curve 1), the UV–visible absorption spectrum of SCDs solution has two wide absorption peaks. There is a significant absorption peak at 277 nm in C = C carbon core, which is caused by the π-π* transition in the aromatic sp2 domain [30]. The luminescence band at 350 nm may be derived from the n-π* leap of C = O that leads to the luminescence of SCDs. Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra shows that the best emission wavelength and excitation wavelength of the prepared SCD are 467 nm and 350 nm (Fig. 2e). The fluorescence spectra of SCDs at various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2f) were investigated. When SCDs were in the excitation wavelength range of 310–410 nm, the emission peak of SCDs at 467 nm almost does not fluctuate, SCDs has excitation-independence, which is attributed to a single sp2 carbon radiative leap [31]. However, the fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength is influenced by the different excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensity of SCDs is maximum when the excitation wavelength is 350 nm. Usually, the excitation dependence is due to the presence of energy traps on the surface of SCDs and different particle sizes. Multiple functional groups on the surface of SCDs can form emission traps at π-π* of C–C [32]. At specific excitation wavelengths, SCDs emission is mainly affected by surface energy traps. At other excitation wavelengths, surface state emission traps also play an important role. In previous reports, the solution for this was to purify the CDs [33]. The SCDs prepared were not purified and had non-dependency, probably because the functional groups such as C-O, C = O, and C-N passivated the surface defected of SCDs after doping.

The surface functional groups and elements of SCDs were analyzed by XPS technique. As observed in Fig. 3 a, SCDs has three characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.2 eV and 530.7 eV, belonging to the characteristic binding energy signals of C1s, N1s and O1s respectively [34]. Elemental analysis shows that SCDs was mainly composed of C, N and O elements, accounting for 64.91% (C), 17.88% (N) and 17.21% (O), respectively. Elemental analysis shows that SCDs is successfully doped with nitrogen and rich in content. The high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) shows three peaks corresponding to C-O group (287.4 eV), O-C = O group (285.5 eV), C = O group (284.4 eV). High resolution O1s spectrograph (Fig. 3c) shows three main peaks corresponding to 532.2 eV in O-C group, 531.5 eV in O = C group and 530.4 eV in C-OH group, respectively. High resolution N1 spectrum (Fig. 3d) shows two main peaks corresponding to 400.3 eV in C-N group and 390.5 eV in N–H group respectively. The above characterization results can indicate the successful synthesis of nitrogen doped SCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions of SCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis conditions of SCDs in this experiment were explored and optimized in order to obtain the optimal fluorescence properties. The synthesis of SCDs using sunflower seed shells as a carbon source has the defect of low fluorescence intensity, which restricts their practical application. Therefore, nitrogen doping was chosen to change the electronic structure and internal properties of SCDs [23] to improve their optical properties and increase quantum yields. The surface of biomass sunflower seed shells is rich in carboxyl functional groups, and the positively charged SCDs can be more easily prepared by passivating the surface of SCDs with PEI.

Firstly, the influence of L-arginine doping amount on the fluorescence intensity and QY of SCDs was studied. When the mass of sunflower seed shells was 1.0 g, the fluorescence intensity and QY of SCDs increased gradually with the increase of L-arginine dosage. When the dosage of L-arginine was 0.2 g, the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum and QY increased to 23.38%. It is noteworthy that the QY of undoped SCDs is only 3.58% (Fig. S2a-b), about 8 times of that before doping. It can be inferred that the surface state of SCDs changes after doping, and a mass of electrons were captured on the surface of SCDs. This increased the radiative recombination of SCDs and improved the fluorescence performance of SCDs [24]. When content of L-arginine was greater than 0.2 g, the QY of SCDs did not increase, and the effective surface traps tended to saturate. Therefore, the optimal doping amount of L-arginine was 0.2 g.

In addition, the effects of temperature and reaction time were also investigated. When the reaction time was 6 h, both the fluorescence intensity and QY of SCDs gradually improved as the reaction temperature increased (from 120 °C to 180 °C); when the temperature increased to 200 °C, the fluorescence intensity and QY decreased relatively (Fig. S2c-d). When the reaction temperature was controlled at 180 °C, the fluorescence intensity and QY of SCDs gradually increased from 2 h to 8 h and decreased from 10 h to 12 h (Fig. S2e-f). This is because the carbon core [25] can effectively form a fluorescent carbon core at a suitable temperature. The reaction precursor was dehydrated and aromatized when the temperature was too high or too long. Large number of non-fluorescent carbon spheres formed, resulting in decreased fluorescence. Altogether, 0.2 g L-arginine, 180 °C and 6 h were the optimal synthesis conditions.

The fluorescence intensity of SCDs at different pH values (pH = 3.0–12.0) was studied (Fig. S3a). The fluorescence intensity of SCDs increased slightly when the pH value was from 3.0 to 6.0, while remained unchanged when the pH value was from 5.0 to 10.0. The fluorescence intensity decreased sharply when the pH value increased from 10.0 to 12.0. The results showed that the material had a wide range of pH regulation. Under weakly alkaline or acidic conditions, protonation and deprotonation will occur in the functional groups on the surface of SCDs, forming a positive or negative protective layer on the surface of SCDs, which protects the structure of SCDs from destruction to a certain extent. However, when the pH exceeded this range in strong alkaline conditions, the protective layer of SCDs was disrupted, leading to a decrease in fluorescence intensity [35].

To determine the stability of SCDs in ionic strength environment, the fluorescence intensity was measured at different concentrations of NaCl. The fluorescence intensity remained substantially unchanged, indicating that SCDs could remain stable under 0–500 mM NaCl ion intensity (Fig. S3b).

To evaluate the fluorescence stability of SCDs, SCDs solution was irradiated at 350 nm UV light for 60 min. As Fig. S3c showed, the fluorescence emission intensity of SCDs remained unchanged. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of SCDs has been remained almost unchanged after storage at room temperature for at least three months. The results show that SCDs nanoparticles has good stability and have broad application prospect in sensing field.

The feasibility of the HPV 16 detection method was investigated by analyzing the effect of different substances in the system on the fluorescence of SCDs. The fluorescence intensity was almost the same as the blank value when HPV 16 was added to SCDs alone (Fig. 4 a). Whereas the fluorescence intensity of SCDs decreased significantly when D-DNA was added solely, demonstrating that the addition of D-DNA was responsible for the fluorescence change. Clearly, the fluorescence of SCDs was only restored after the simultaneous addition of D-DNA and HPV 16. Therefore, this method for detecting HPV 16 was feasible.

The detection of HPV 16 was performed under optimal experimental conditions, as shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of SCDs decreased with increasing D-DNA concentration, and the fluorescence of SCDs was quenched to the maximum by D-DNA at a concentration of 300 nM. The fluorescence intensity ratio showed a good linear relationship when the concentration range of D-DNA was from 10 to 300 nM. The quenching of SCDs by D-DNA was in accordance with the Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F = Kq·[Q] + 1, where F or F0 indicates the fluorescence intensity in the presence or absence of D-DNA, Kq and [C] are the quenching constant and concentration. The regression equation of D-DNA and SCDs: (F0/F) = 1.0351 + 0.0090CD-DNA, the regression parameter (R2) of the linear response curve was 0.994 (Fig. 4c). F0 is the fluorescence intensity before the addition of D-DNA, F is after the addition of D-DNA. CD-DNA is the concentration of D-DNA. Likewise, the established “on” fluorescence technique was applied to the detection of HPV 16. D-DNA was complementary to the specific bases of HPV 16. A fluorescent “on” system for sensitive detection of HPV 16 was constructed on this basis. Under optimal experimental conditions, a range of HPV 16 concentrations was determined (Fig. 4d). The ratio showed a good linear relationship of fluorescence intensity when the HPV 16 concentration was in the range of 0.5–150 nM (Fig. 4e). The linear relationship met the regression equation: (F0/F) = 0.9636–0.0017CHPV16. The regression parameter (R2) of the linear response curve was 0.995. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of SCDs before and after the addition of HPV 16, respectively. CHPV16 indicates the concentration of HPV 16. The detection limit of HPV 16 calculated based on 3σ/ K was 0.47 nM, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample signal and K is the slope of this response curve. The relative standard deviation for 11 repeated assessments was 3.4%.

In order to investigate the influence of D-DNA on SCDs during fluorescence quenching, the effect of reaction time on the experimental process was studied. As can be seen from Fig. S4a, the fluorescence intensity of SCDs under the action of D-DNA began to decline within 5 min, and decreased to the maximum at 35 min, while the fluorescence intensity remained unchanged. Therefore, 35 min was selected as the optimal reaction incubation time. Similarly, in the process of fluorescence recovery after the addition of HPV 16, fluorescence recovery reached the maximum after 40 min (Fig. S4b). Therefore, it revealed that 40 min was the best reaction condition for detecting HPV 16.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity analysis for detection of HPV 16 assays
ce:section.ce:para: The selective evaluation of the HPV 16 assay developed in this experiment was made. Different number of base mismatched of ssDNA (1, 3, 6 pairs), anions, amino acids, and biomolecules were introduced into the detection system. As shown in the Fig. 4f, the results of the control experiments showed a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity as the number of base mismatch increased. When there was a 1-base mismatch in ssDNA, the proposed fluorescence sensor responded to it, because the ssDNA was similar to HPV16 in structure, but only one base was different. However, in actual samples, the concentration of 1-base mismatch ssDNA is often very low, or even only exists in theoretical studies, so it has little influence in practical analysis. After the addition of metal ions, small amino acids and large molecules BSA, the fluorescence intensity of SCDs-D-DNA probe were equivalent to the blank value. Ultimately, the SCDs-D-DNA fluorescent probe can be used for the quantitative analysis of HPV DNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of HPV16 in actual urine and cervical swab samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the fluorescent probe in actual urine and cervical swabs samples obtained from Qinggang Chinese Medicine Hospital was tested. The calculated results were shown in Table S2-S3.

The results of the established method for detecting HPV16 in urine and cervical swab samples were 17.8, 12.3, 18.4 nmol·L−1 and 52.5, 54.2 and 65.7 nmol·L−1, respectively. The recoveries of urine and cervical swabs were 96.6%-113.3% and 95.7%-103.9%, respectively. RSD were 2.5%-3.8% and 2.6%-3.5%, respectively. For comparative analysis, we validated the test results with well-established assay methods based on nanodrop spectrophotometry [36] reported in the literature, which in urine samples were 18.1, 12.7 and 18.2 nmol·L−1 and in cervical swabs samples were 53.4, 55.1 and 65.2 nmol·L−1. As Table S2-S3 showed, the obtained results from the established method are consistent with the common standard method for determination of DNA concentration. It is proved that our proposed method has certain accuracy. Through the analysis of the above results, the fluorescence method for detecting HPV 16 had reliable precision and accuracy. In addition, our established HPV16 detection method has good specificity.

The method was analyzed in comparison with other HPV DNA detection methods (Table 1 ), which showed similar recovery and RSDs compared to some reported methods. The LOD we tested was comparable or even better. It had a good linearity in the range of 0.5–150 nM for the determination of HPV 16 at low concentrations. In addition, in terms of material selection, it is easy to prepare SCDs from sunflower seed shells with low cost, environmental protection and low toxicity. Furthermore, the method has good selectivity and stability. It is easy to operate and does not require complex and expensive instruments, which has a broad application prospect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching and recovery mechanism of SCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching mechanism of SCDs fluorescence by D-DNA was studied. In the construction of DNA sensing probe, dark Dabcyl quenching agent was selected as modification group to quench carbon point fluorescence, which is very effective in inhibiting various fluorescent materials from blue to green emission spectrum [37]. The quenching effect is the best in the range of 400–550 nm, and the maximum absorption value is 453 nm. It overlaps the fluorescence emission peak (470 nm) of SCDs, so we speculate that the quenching mechanism is likely to be Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and internal filtering [38,39]. In order to determine whether it is Förster resonance energy transfer or internal filtering, the UV absorption spectra of the probe before and after the reaction with SCDs are shown in the Fig. 5 a. It showed no change in the UV absorption peaks before and after the addition of D-DNA. Also measured the fluorescence lifetimes of SCDs before and after the addition of the quencher D-DNA, respectively. The average fluorescence lifetime were 5.49 ns and 4.41 ns, respectively (Fig. 5c-d). The fluorescence lifetimes were changed before and after the addition of D-DNA. The quenching mechanism may be attributed to FRET. The attraction between SCDs and D-DNA under the conditions of this experiment was studied. After D-DNA was added to SCDs solution, the fluorescence intensity of SCDs decreased first and then almost remained stable with the increase of D-DNA concentration (Fig. S5a). In addition, the changes of zeta potential before and after fluorescence quenching was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 5b, the surface of SCDs was negatively charged and the measured potential was −13.89 mV, it was functionalized by PEI surface and the positively charged potential changed to 26.34 mV, when D-DNA was added, the fluorescence intensity decreased while the potential changed to −4.52 mV. In particular, the potential showed (Fig. S5b) that the SCDs surface was positively charged, and the solution potential decreased as the concentration of negatively charged D-DNA increased. Therefore, D-DNA could bind to SCDs by electrostatic adsorption.

Then, the fluorescence “on” mechanism of the DNA probe was studied. After adding various concentrations of HPV 16 into the reaction system, the fluorescence gradually recovered, the potential value changed from −4.52 mV to 13.77 mV, proving that that the base complementary pairing hybridization occurred between D-DNA and HPV 16, D-DNA detached from the SCDs surface, and the free SCDs increased in the solution, and the fluorescence recovered.

In this study, SCDs with excellent optical properties were prepared using waste sunflower seed shells as precursors, and a fast and simple “off-on” fluorescence strategy was established. It fully reflects the reuse of biomass energy and solves the disadvantages of high cost and toxicity of traditional fluorescent materials. Based on DNA molecular hybridization technique, we constructed DNA probe and combined it with the prepared SCDs, which was successfully applied to the detection of viral DNA sequences. With the advantages of high sensitivity and specificity, rapid analysis, miniaturization and simplicity, it has great potential for practical application. We will proceed to develop a wider range of applications for this material in the future.

All applicable international, national, and/or institutional guidelines for the collection and use of human blood and serum samples were followed. All biological experiments were approved by the Ethics Committee of Harbin Medical University Cancer Hospital.

Xinxin Jiang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Xinjian Liu: Data curation, Investigation. Meng Wu: Investigation. Yanmei Ma: Conceptualization. Xiaoyu Xu: Software. Ligang Chen: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Na Niu: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was funded by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572021BU03), 111 Project (B20088), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107742.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
